findings.message: D2 requires the results command to list fail/pending pairs with claim/evidence source locations, but D3 makes those source locations available only by adding origin metadata to verify-cache entries and later says results can work from the cache alone. That does not cover pending/cache-miss pairs, which have no cache entry to carry origin metadata, so an implementation following this plan either cannot show pending pair sources or must re-load the current pair source/artifacts despite the stated cache-only design. The plan needs to explicitly choose one model: persist pending entries with origins, or have `results` derive pending pairs from the source artifacts.

`RefVerifyCommand` still documents the new variant as `Results(ResultsArgs)`, but this artifact defines the type as `RefVerifyResultsArgs` and the variant payload as `Results(RefVerifyResultsArgs)`. That broken reference will mislead anyone consuming the generated type docs.

T002 names the synthetic origin for `make_known_bad_probe` as `GoalSection`, but T001 only introduces `SpecSectionKind::Goal` and does not define any `GoalSection` symbol. As written, the task refers to a non-existent variant/type.

The results-record contract requires a `reason` field in each pair row, but `RefVerifyPairRecord` only carries hashes, verdict, chain scope, and origins, and T005's renderer only prints those fields. Without a `reason` source in the data model, IN-03 / AC-03 cannot be satisfied as written.

The default verdict filter is specified inconsistently. This file says `chain` and `filter` both default to `All`, but `impl-plan.json` T006 sets `--filter` to default to `fail` and its no-args test expects `filter=fail`. Implementers will get conflicting guidance on the command's baseline output.

`acceptance_criterion` is the wrong source key in the Chain-1 mapping. The rest of this track consistently uses `acceptance_criteria` / `AcceptanceCriteria`, so this task would leave the acceptance-criteria section unmapped.

T002 says the schema migration updates `SemanticVerifyEntryDto`, but the infrastructure catalog/contract-map in this artifact set never model that DTO at all. That leaves the phase-2 and phase-3 artifacts inconsistent about whether the cache DTO is part of the track.

IN-03 describes the record block as a fail/pending-only enumeration, but AC-09 and the implementation plan also require `pass` rows to be emitted when `--filter pass` or `--filter all` is used. The artifacts never settle on whether pass rows belong in the record block.

AC-07/AC-08 are CLI-level behaviors, but the coverage matrix only assigns them to T003/T004/T005 and omits T006/T007, where the `Results` subcommand and the `--chain`/`--layer` parsing and dispatch wiring are actually introduced. As written, the matrix can mark those acceptance criteria covered before the CLI layer exists.

`find_spec_element_text(...).unwrap_or_default()` silently fabricates an empty `text_label` if the spec lookup fails, but `IN-07` requires the origin to carry the verbatim spec text. That would let Chain-2 evidence origins be recorded without the source text they are supposed to preserve, so this path needs to error instead of defaulting.

`T005` still turns every non-`all` `--layer` value into `LayerId::try_new(...)` and fails on validation errors, but AC-08 says `--layer` is a no-op when `--chain 1` is selected. As written, `sotp ref-verify results --chain 1 --layer <invalid>` would reject instead of ignoring the layer flag.

AC-07/AC-08 define `--chain` and `--layer` as limiting the displayed result set, but the implementation plan and output DTO require `total_pass/fail/pending` to be global totals across all chains and layers. With `--chain 1` or `--layer domain`, the Summary line would include counts for lanes the command otherwise excludes, contradicting the filter semantics and producing misleading results. The plan should define totals over the chain/layer-filtered lane set while remaining unaffected only by the verdict filter.

`SemanticVerifyEntryDto` is marked with `action: "add"`, but the same entry says T002 modifies the existing DTO by adding required `claim_origin` and `evidence_origin` fields. This contradicts the schema-migration plan and can send implementers down the wrong path of adding a new DTO instead of modifying the cache-entry DTO in place.

`RefVerifyDriver` is marked as `action: "reference"`, while the entry itself says the match body gains a `Results` arm in this track. T005 also requires adding `ref_verify_results` dispatch behavior, so the catalogue action should reflect a modification rather than a reference-only dependency.

`RefVerifyResultsArgs` defines the `--filter` default as `Fail`, but the spec's default command acceptance criterion requires the record block to include fail/pending pairs. Since AC-09 says `--filter fail` shows only fail pairs, the planned default `sotp ref-verify results` output would omit pending records and fail AC-01/ADR D2's default-output intent.

T002 explicitly modifies the usecase `RefVerifyPair` shape by adding `claim_origin` and `evidence_origin`, but its catalogue refs omit any usecase catalogue entry and `usecase-types.json` contains no `RefVerifyPair` entry. That leaves the cross-layer schema migration's usecase contract uncatalogued while domain `SemanticVerifyEntry` and infrastructure `SemanticVerifyEntryDto` are catalogued, so the plan artifacts disagree about the full D3 contract surface.

T003 extends `RefVerifyAggregateService` with a new required `results` method while explicitly leaving the concrete `FsRefVerifyAggregateAdapter` implementation to T004. Since the catalogue still has that adapter implementing the trait, doing T003 as a standalone task leaves the Rust trait impl incomplete until T004, so the planned task boundary is not executable as an independently compilable step. Either make T003/T004 atomic like T002 or include a compile-safe trait implementation/default in T003.

AC-12 requires `bin/sotp ref-verify run` to return a non-zero error when it reads an old cache, but CN-05/IN-08 in the same spec says old caches are regenerated by rerunning `ref-verify run`. A run that fails on the old cache cannot also regenerate it unless the plan adds a delete/overwrite path, so the old-cache handling contract is internally inconsistent.

T006 makes the no-args default `--filter all`, while AC-01 defines the record block as fail/pending pairs. With this default, ordinary `sotp ref-verify results` output includes pass records whenever they exist, so the planned CLI behavior no longer matches the stated default record-block contract. Clarify whether default output is fail+pending only or all pairs.

`impl-plan.json` moves T001-T003 from `todo` to `done`, but the batch brief explicitly says this round should not change `impl-plan.json` at all; only the rendered plan view should reflect the task transitions.

`CatalogueEntryKey` is still marked yellow even though T001 is marked complete and the other T001-added domain types in this file are blue, so the domain-type artifact is internally inconsistent about whether batch A finished the whole domain slice.

This rendered catalogue still marks the three new CLI artifacts introduced by T006 (`RefVerifyChainArg`, `RefVerifyVerdictFilterArg`, `RefVerifyResultsArgs`) as red, while the plan/impl-plan mark T006 as done. That makes the plan set internally inconsistent about whether the CLI results surface is complete.

The `RefVerifyError` docs say `results` and `check-approved` return `Result<_, RefVerifyError>`, but the same catalogue defines `RefVerifyAggregateService::check_approved` and `::results` as returning `Result<..., RefVerifyDriverError>`. This leaves the plan artifact internally contradictory about the usecase error contract and can send implementers/tests toward the wrong public API type.

Both `chain` and `filter` are custom `ValueEnum`s, but these fields are missing `#[arg(value_enum)]`. As written, clap will not wire the enum parser here, so valid inputs like `--chain 1` and `--filter pass` will not be accepted by `ref-verify results`.

Chain-2 evidence origins are built from `find_spec_element_text`, which returns the rendered snippet (`[section id] text`) rather than the raw spec element text. That violates the `SpecElementRef` contract that `text_label` is verbatim text, so the cache will persist a different origin payload than Chain-1 and the codec/domain model expect.

`load_results_tddd_bindings` falls back to `Ok(Vec::new())` when `architecture-rules.json` is malformed or absent. This helper feeds the results adapter's Chain-2 cache loading, so a broken rules file can be treated as “no Chain-2 layers” instead of failing closed. The lower-level `load_tddd_layers` contract treats this file as required because catalogue bindings cannot be trusted without it; per the infrastructure severity policy, config loaders must not silently default on parse/missing-file errors when the convention is fail-closed.

For `--chain 1`, this still calls `RefVerifyPairSourceAdapter::load_pairs` with the resolver's unfiltered `scope`, which is `All` for normal tracks. That path enumerates Chain2 pairs and reads/parses `architecture-rules.json`, so a malformed rules file or broken Chain2 catalogue can still fail a Chain1-only results query before `compute_results` gets the data. The new cache-loading short-circuit needs the same Chain1-only treatment for pair enumeration, or it should call a Chain1-only source path here.

Calling `load_results_tddd_bindings` here makes `results --chain All` require `architecture-rules.json` even in the documented pre-TDDD / pre-architecture-rules state where Chain2 should contribute zero pairs. The existing scope resolver and Chain2 pair source treat a missing rules file as "no TDDD layers declared"; this branch now returns an error instead of Chain1-only results.

The `All` branch uses `filter_map(...).ok()` when building `target_ids`, so any `architecture-rules.json` layer id that is accepted by `parse_tddd_layers` but rejected by `LayerId::try_new` is silently skipped instead of failing closed. The previous `enumerate_chain2_all_layers` path surfaced this as a `Wiring` error, so `results --chain All` can now under-report an entire layer without any signal.

`load_results_tddd_bindings` now converts a missing `architecture-rules.json` into `Ok(Vec::new())`, so a Chain2 results request with `--layer all` can silently report zero Chain2 lanes instead of failing closed when the layer binding config is absent. This overrides the underlying `load_tddd_layers` contract, which treats the missing rules file as a hard configuration error because catalogue bindings cannot be determined without it. Please propagate the `NotFound` as `RefVerifyDriverError::Wiring` for Chain2 paths instead of defaulting to an empty binding set.

When the request is for a specific Chain2 layer whose catalogue is absent in the pre-Phase-2 state, this new skip leaves `chain2_caches` and `chain2_layer_ids` empty, but `results()` still passes the original `RefVerifyLayerFilter::Specific(layer)` into `compute_results`. `compute_results` validates `Specific` against `chain2_caches` and returns `Wiring` when the layer is missing there, so `--chain 2 --layer <valid-layer>` still fails instead of producing the intended zero-pair result. The graceful skip needs to preserve the fact that the layer was a valid target, or bypass the duplicate cache-based validation for this absent-catalogue case.

The partial-catalogue fail-closed check only inspects `target_ids`, so `--chain 2 --layer domain` can still silently succeed in a partial Chain2 state. Example: `architecture-rules.json` declares `domain` and `usecase`, `usecase-types.json` exists, and `domain-types.json` is missing. With a specific `domain` filter, this loop records only `domain` as absent, `present` stays empty, `check_partial_catalogue_set` treats it as the all-absent pre-Phase-2 case, and results returns zero pairs instead of a Wiring error. The presence/absence scan needs to classify all declared Chain2 catalogues before applying the layer narrowing, matching the fail-closed partial-set policy.

This makes `results --chain 1` fail on a valid Phase-0 track that has `track/items/<track_id>/` but no `spec.json`. `pair_source.rs` explicitly treats a missing `spec.json` as the legal zero-pair case, so this helper turns that state into `Wiring` instead of letting Chain1 return an empty summary.

`--chain 1` now bypasses `RefVerifyScopeResolver::resolve` entirely, so the IN-06 consistency check is no longer enforced on the Chain1-only path. A track with catalogues present but no `spec.json` will now fall through to empty Chain1 output instead of returning a typed `Wiring` error, which silently accepts an inconsistent on-disk state.

`load_chain1_scope_tddd_bindings_or_empty()` converts both `LoadTdddLayersError::Parse` and `LoadTdddLayersError::Io(NotFound)` into `Ok(Vec::new())`, so a malformed or missing `architecture-rules.json` is treated as "no TDDD bindings". That makes `--chain 1` silently succeed instead of surfacing the config error, which defeats the fail-closed contract established by `load_tddd_layers` and can hide broken Phase-2 configuration behind an empty-result path.

`resolve_chain1_only_scope()` now hard-fails on `load_tddd_layers()` errors, including `architecture-rules.json` being absent. That makes `ref-verify results --chain 1` reject pre-TDDD / zero-layer repositories, even though the existing resolver and `load_results_tddd_bindings()` treat a missing rules file as "no TDDD layers". Chain1-only scope resolution should not introduce a stricter config dependency than the rest of the ref-verify path.

`check_track_dir_exists()` only calls `try_exists()` on `track/items/<track_id>`. That accepts a symlink or even a regular file at the track slot, so the new precheck does not actually reject a non-real track directory. For `--chain 2`-only results this can still fall through to an empty success instead of the `Wiring` error the change is trying to enforce. The existing `enumerate_chain1_pairs()` guard shows the needed pattern: reject symlinks and confirm `is_dir()`.

Step 0b still says the per-scope ceiling is a "hard upper bound at split time". That preserves the old hard-cap semantics and conflicts with the new advisory behavior in Rule 3, where over-ceiling singleton batches are explicitly allowed to proceed.

Step 1b now defines ceiling overflow as advisory and says to log and continue, but the DFP `completed` branch still gates Review on the batch fitting the ceiling. An over-ceiling singleton that Rule 3 explicitly permits will pass Step 1b, then be blocked here before Review, so the command still behaves like a hard cap.

The Review/DFP fixpoint still requires `actual diff stays within ceiling` as a precondition for Commit. That contradicts the new advisory overflow behavior in Step 1b and makes any legitimate over-ceiling atomic batch impossible to finish after review fixes, reintroducing the hard-cap semantics the change is meant to remove.
